text:
Hi, Bridge.
Hey, how's it going?
Good, thanks.
How are you doing?
Well, Dan, give me one second.
I am just getting back to my desk after making a coffee.
How's your morning?
Good.
I'm actually in London, so it's already the afternoon here.
Oh, nice.
Do you live in London full time?
Sort of.
Bit unclear.
I'm always on the go.
My wife lives here, but.
I'm in denial that I live here as someone that has lived the nomad life for at least a good part of, of, of his existence.
Yeah, I feel that it's, it's home is where I am, is what I like to think, or where my loved ones are.
So, you know, it's a flexible concept.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, hey, thanks so much for, for jumping on.
It was, you know, really cool hearing about, hearing about your company during the McKinsey sort of founder time.
And obviously you'd mentioned that you're growing really fast and so wanted to both be generally helpful and sort of understand, you know, what, what are the pain points maybe you're going through are the ways that I can help, either directly through my company or just indirectly, but then also, you know, it's a great learning exercise for me to speak to folks that are scaling up their teams.
Nice.
Yeah, very cool.
And by the way, I don't know if you have multiple cameras, but I see it from the side in case you have one.
I do.
Yeah.
That is, Let me fix that.
Hey, there we are.
Are we there?
There we are.
Yeah.
Yeah.
These, these, these office setups, man, they're getting too, too elaborate these days.
Yeah, yeah, very cool.
And look, I, I, I admit I didn't have time to, to look at your company because I'm actually in term sheet negotiations this week and it's been absolutely mental.
Oh, man.
So I'm like, just texting here with, with the lawyers in parallel.
No, no.
So understand that.
How's that going, by the way?
Are you feeling good about it?
Yeah, we have a lead that we're pretty excited about, so.
Amazing.
Amazing.
Oh, man, that's half the battle, right?
Getting that first person to really stake out their, their support.
Exactly, exactly.
So super exciting.
And yeah, that means we're going to be hiring start of next year.
Definitely.
Interesting.
What have you, what have you sort of planned out for that hiring so far?
And how are you feeling about going into that hiring cycle?
Yeah, we haven't, we haven't really planned out much other than like roughly the profiles that we need, which is like kind of an AI engineer front end Engineer and a full stack engineer.
Sounds about right.
Yep.
Pretty standard.
Like first team.
Yeah.
Yep.
Set up.
So that's, that's mostly what we need.
Probably like some a part time UX designer or something like that.
And then I'm going to handle most of the commercial stuff.
Maybe like get a founder's associate, something like that as well to help me a bit.
But yeah, that's, that's kind of it.
That's the main ones.
And are there any concerns, pain points I didn't note?
If you're a second time founder, usually those folks are a lot more chilled out about hiring but wanted to get a sense of sort of where you're, what headspace you're in and sort of what if there are any sort of anxieties or concerns that are worth addressing?
Good question.
I mean, I think, you know, it's, it feels like it's a competitive market out there.
It's hard to say, right.
Like on the one hand it's super competitive.
On the other hand a ton of people are being let go.
So.
And it feels paradoxical, right?
It feels paradoxical that that would be, that would be the case.
Do you have any, do you have a suspicion of why that might be?
Well, I think, yeah.
I mean obviously like with AI, right, the engineers are becoming a lot more productive and actually the people that are getting let go of are probably the less good ones.
Right.
So it almost like makes it worse because the people that are going to be applying are going to be the one who, you know, it's not always true, right, that the people that let.
Go are the ones.
But like tendencies.
There's probably something that the companies are doing where they're like assessing who to let go of and who to not.
So yeah, I would, I would largely agree with that.
I would say that is, that is one of three things that I would point out to you.
One is certainly that phenomenon, right, where obviously the less performant folks are the ones that are let go first second dimension is something everyone's familiar with.
The best people aren't looking for jobs, right?
They're passive and they getting through to them or getting eyeballs on them is naturally very hard.
So that's the second dimension of a problem.
The founders I speak to, it's like, hey, I know I have a cool company, I know I have a good job, I need to get better eyeballs on it.
That's the sort of second dimension of the problem.
Usually you tap out your referral network pretty early by the seed stage.
You know, it's rare that people have that many cracked engineers in their network.
Yeah.
So that's the second problem, but the third is completely relates to Inbound.
Have you done any sort of Inbound hiring recently?
No, no, we haven't done any hiring yet.
We're super early.
It's just a co founder, Ryan.
I just so Inbound is a fucking mess.
It's an absolute mess.
And it's a mess because ATS systems are no longer able to differentiate between candidates.
Right.
When the marginal cost of bullshit is 0 and an AI can perfectly fabricate a resume, it means resumes are completely pointless.
A signal that combined with Auto Apply is, you know, effectively we're seeing folks get buried in applications, but founders are spending more time reading resumes and doing screenings than ever.
And that's the main sort of thing that, that we address or we, we sort of try to help folks with.
Do you have any sense of how much you're going to rely on inbound or are you going more with a referral based strategy?
Yeah, referral.
Or even hiring a recruiter in the worst case.
Yeah, yeah, fair enough, Fair enough.
Yeah.
I mean, I think, I think those are all legitimate options if you're interested in a recruiter.
We have a bunch of recruiting is.
I don't know if.
Oh, yeah, we haven't announced this officially.
I don't think it's come out in the network newsletter.
But my company, Careerspan, we signed a deal with Marvin to be an official talent partner for them.
And so as part of that, all community members get, you know, first hundred applicants on the house.
As far as going through Career Span, getting processed by Careerspan doesn't seem like you're too enthusiastic about Inbound.
And I will say there's a reason for that.
Like, it's sucks.
But the thing that we do that's very different is the way in which applicants engage with Career Span is a lot more substantive and lends itself to better signal.
So amongst all of these different strategies that you're using, good hiring is like a diet.
And you want to make Inbound a meaningful part of the diet.
We help you do that without having to then also spend a ton of your time and Energy reviewing 99 resumes for one winner, which is really the problem with Inbound.
Cool.
Well, do you want to tell me a little bit more about how you guys work and.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, absolutely.
So where Careerspan been?
Been at it for about two years.
Spent most of our existence as a career coaching product.
The pain we identified was that people don't need resume generators, they don't need interview help.
Really what they need is something to help them understand their value proposition.
For most folks, the reason they suck at job hunting is their own inability to be good advocates for themselves.
Right?
The resume is a byproduct of your.
Is a, is a byproduct of your ability to self advocate.
So our tool uses used conversational coaching to help people develop that ability.
In the process, what we learned is that that modality is actually really powerful at getting to know someone.
And the interview process is essentially a bad compromise between getting to know someone and evaluating them.
So by using Career Span, you get to get people to engage in this pretty cognitively intense process that gives you better insight into who they are.
But then also that all happens at scale with an AI.
So you only see the actionable insights off of all of that effort they're putting in.
Say that last two sentence again.
Sorry.
So you basically get the actionable insights, that is, why should I talk to this person?
Why shouldn't I talk to this person?
You get those insights, you know, by virtue of them having engaged in this more extended interaction with Career Span, which is something that you have to figure out when you read a resume.
Right?
When you read a resume, you are problem solving and trying to figure out whether this person's worth talking to.
Right.
Our product gives you that actionable information with the reasoning traces so that if you wanted to justify that, you could look back and see why the AI is, is saying what it's saying.
Okay, got it.
So.
So if I understand correctly, basically for job hunters, you're offering a still to like help them develop their value proposition.
And in the process of engaging with your platform to develop that value proposition, you basically create a sort of profile of this person, which then is used by hirers to assess or like to figure out who would be a good match.
Precisely.
In fact, I can show you visually as well what that ends up looking like is actually this.
So in this account, I mean, very similar to an ATS ostensibly.
But what you get with Careerspan you don't get anywhere else is obviously summary of the candidate, but you get to see how many stories they've told.
Each story is about 15 minutes worth of effort, generally answering about 7 to 10 pretty detailed, thought provoking questions about your experience.
So if you can only imagine how much data that collects for you.
And then to my point about actionability, we're planning on renaming these, but this is essentially why should you say yes to Donna?
Why should you say no To Donna.
Right.
You can make a decision much faster off of this.
Oh, sorry.
I think someone wants to join the call.
Oh, thanks.
That's my note taker.
Yeah, of course.
So I assumed.
Yeah.
So you, you essentially get in these very actionable terms and I can actually spin up this account for you.
So we can make dummy accounts pretty easily.
I can spin up this account for you and get this out, but it, it sort of is able to give you this much higher grade of analysis.
The other thing you'll observe is these are the same person.
It's just with this, it was only the resume and this is resume plus stories.
And so you can, you know, even if you just want to for now, take my word on the fact that 62 to versus 90 means something that shows you the delta in nuance data that we are able to bring to the table to help you refine your decision.
Yeah, interesting.
Yeah, very interesting.
The other reason why I wanted to talk to you earlier than even when you put out the job is, you know, let's face it, if you put this job out publicly, you give away your hand to your, your sort of competitors, you, you reveal your hand and the longer you have it out, the more resumes you have to go through.
Right.
Or you have to read.
It's completely pointless.
At the same time, wouldn't it be nice for you to have a pipeline, right.
At least a frame of reference to begin the search?
With careerspan, you can have both.
You can put your job in as a stealth job onto our job board and just start aggregating users.
We're seeing generally about 100 hits a day, but we only launched the job board about two, three weeks ago.
So it's, you know, you're going to see that ramp up.
It'll be completely cloaked.
So you don't, you don't have to have any sort of additional work obligation arising and we will just collect up the candidates for you and just give them to you when you actually start looking.
So it's, it's a way to like trawl for candidates almost, you know, in the sea.
And so you're that that phase is candidates applying to that stealth thing or is that you guys not revealing the job posting at all and you picking off people that you think are more most relevant.
The former, the former us putting up the job as a stealth job, making it clear that the hiring is going to begin, let's say in Feb.
Right.
That transparency is good, we recommend.
But the thing is, in any other dynamic, if it was just Submitting a resume, there's no real reason for the person to do that.
Right.
In the case of Career Span because we have a pretty highly engaged user base.
On average, the activated CareerSpan user does two and a half stories.
That's almost.
Yeah, that's almost 40, 50 minutes worth of self reflection that they do that drives the sort of matching experience.
And so what we've observed is because people already have those stories.
Anyone that's a good fit for your job.
They can truly one click apply but you will still only see the personalized interpretation of why they aren't a fit.
Okay, very interesting.
Nice.
Yeah.
Are the folks using your platform primarily.
Geographically concentrated anywhere us Most of our candidates are unsurprisingly New York, Boston, sf and that's where most of our roles are as well.
We are seeing a lot of demand for remote roles.
So if you are posting a remote role, we generally see greater uptake for that.
But we've got some awesome companies.
We've got.
Probably the coolest is like Zoe, they're a lightspeed backed startup, 8 million seed round.
They just found their infrastructure engineer who is a. I love this story because this is just a guy in Bozeman, Montana, right?
Just a guy in Bozeman, Montana applied through Career Span.
You know, no great shakes University would not have gotten a look in on any other platform.
Right.
On Career Span, not only is he brought to the front of the table, it's good for Zo because they get an underpriced asset.
I'm sure we all like that when we're hiring, right?
You know who, who doesn't want messy on a budget, right?
That's, that's sort of what we're.
That's also part of the framing is like you get this money ballist aspect with your hiring.
You know the last thing I'll say.
And this is just good advice from the hiring perspective.
Studies show that 46% of hires fail by the 18 month mark.
90% of the reason they fail is a values mindset or soft skills mismatch.
And that probably relates to your own intuitions around hiring that it's the person that ultimately affects success.
Right.
It's not the skills.
Right.
Usually we're pretty good at figuring out who can or can't do the job.
We're very bad at figuring out who can do the job for my team and my company.
And that's perhaps the most important thing that we can help you with.
What percent of people fail by the 18 month mark?
89% according to the Leadership IQ Institute.
Wow.
Right.
Because if you think about it, our whole interviewing evaluation process, Leetcode whiteboard tests, we're very good at assessing skills, hard skills.
We're very bad at assessing vibes and fit and relatability.
The cool thing with us is that because we use a purely semantic evaluation process, you can say, hey, I want someone empathetic.
And our system will find someone whose stories exhibit that quality.
Quality, you know, that's.
No one else can really offer that except us.
So, yeah, I think for.
For those reasons we can, you know, put the job out there.
You get it for free anyways, until the first, like the 101st first candidate.
And we can just hoover up these folks for you.
And then, you know, the moment if you start seeing some folks you're interested in, great, you can talk to them.
But I wanted to come at you with something that was like, you know, next to zero effort and zero sort of additional obligation for you.
Yeah, very cool.
Okay.
Yeah, that makes a ton of sense.
I mean, I think.
Definitely curious to try it, right, and see.
Just see what's on the market and sort of stuff.
I'm going to send you something.
Look at it after your term sheet is signed.
Like, frankly, it isn't even worth thinking about until then.
I'll worry about the following up.
If you're just okay with me prodding you in, you know, later in.
In the year or start of next year.
But yeah, I would just love to be helpful and wishing you the very best of luck, man.
If there's anything else I can clarify as well, let me know.
And if I can be helpful in any way, let me know.
Yeah, super.
Yeah, really appreciate it.
I mean, sounds super cool and really awesome.
Kind of different angle at hiring market that you.
You guys are doing.
So I love that.
I think it's very smart.
Yeah, we'd love to, you know, as.
As it becomes relevant for us, we'd love to do it.
Definitely start of next year is when that's going to be.
So, yeah, please do follow up and yeah, would love to.
Whatever you share is always useful and then we'd love to try it early next year.
Sounds good.
Sounds good.
Well, you take care, Dan, and thank you so much for your time.
I'll catch you soon.
Yeah, likewise.
Well done.
Ciao.
Take care.
Ciao.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Hi, Bridge.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Hey, how's it going?

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Good, thanks.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: How are you doing?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Well, Dan, give me one second.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I am just getting back to my desk after making a coffee.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: How's your morning?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: I'm actually in London, so it's already the afternoon here.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Oh, nice.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Do you live in London full time?

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Sort of.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Bit unclear.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: I'm always on the go.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: My wife lives here, but.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I'm in denial that I live here as someone that has lived the nomad life for at least a good part of, of, of his existence.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah, I feel that it's, it's home is where I am, is what I like to think, or where my loved ones are.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So, you know, it's a flexible concept.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Well, hey, thanks so much for, for jumping on.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It was, you know, really cool hearing about, hearing about your company during the McKinsey sort of founder time.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And obviously you'd mentioned that you're growing really fast and so wanted to both be generally helpful and sort of understand, you know, what, what are the pain points maybe you're going through are the ways that I can help, either directly through my company or just indirectly, but then also, you know, it's a great learning exercise for me to speak to folks that are scaling up their teams.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Nice.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, very cool.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: And by the way, I don't know if you have multiple cameras, but I see it from the side in case you have one.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That is, Let me fix that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Hey, there we are.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Are we there?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: There we are.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: These, these, these office setups, man, they're getting too, too elaborate these days.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, very cool.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: And look, I, I, I admit I didn't have time to, to look at your company because I'm actually in term sheet negotiations this week and it's been absolutely mental.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Oh, man.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: So I'm like, just texting here with, with the lawyers in parallel.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: No, no.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So understand that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: How's that going, by the way?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Are you feeling good about it?

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, we have a lead that we're pretty excited about, so.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Amazing.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Amazing.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Oh, man, that's half the battle, right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Getting that first person to really stake out their, their support.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Exactly, exactly.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: So super exciting.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: And yeah, that means we're going to be hiring start of next year.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Definitely.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Interesting.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: What have you, what have you sort of planned out for that hiring so far?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And how are you feeling about going into that hiring cycle?

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, we haven't, we haven't really planned out much other than like roughly the profiles that we need, which is like kind of an AI engineer front end Engineer and a full stack engineer.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Sounds about right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Pretty standard.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Like first team.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Set up.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: So that's, that's mostly what we need.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Probably like some a part time UX designer or something like that.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: And then I'm going to handle most of the commercial stuff.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Maybe like get a founder's associate, something like that as well to help me a bit.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: But yeah, that's, that's kind of it.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That's the main ones.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And are there any concerns, pain points I didn't note?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: If you're a second time founder, usually those folks are a lot more chilled out about hiring but wanted to get a sense of sort of where you're, what headspace you're in and sort of what if there are any sort of anxieties or concerns that are worth addressing?

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Good question.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: I mean, I think, you know, it's, it feels like it's a competitive market out there.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: It's hard to say, right.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Like on the one hand it's super competitive.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: On the other hand a ton of people are being let go.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And it feels paradoxical, right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It feels paradoxical that that would be, that would be the case.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Do you have any, do you have a suspicion of why that might be?

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Well, I think, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: I mean obviously like with AI, right, the engineers are becoming a lot more productive and actually the people that are getting let go of are probably the less good ones.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: So it almost like makes it worse because the people that are going to be applying are going to be the one who, you know, it's not always true, right, that the people that let.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Go are the ones.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: But like tendencies.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: There's probably something that the companies are doing where they're like assessing who to let go of and who to not.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So yeah, I would, I would largely agree with that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I would say that is, that is one of three things that I would point out to you.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: One is certainly that phenomenon, right, where obviously the less performant folks are the ones that are let go first second dimension is something everyone's familiar with.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The best people aren't looking for jobs, right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: They're passive and they getting through to them or getting eyeballs on them is naturally very hard.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So that's the second dimension of a problem.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The founders I speak to, it's like, hey, I know I have a cool company, I know I have a good job, I need to get better eyeballs on it.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That's the sort of second dimension of the problem.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Usually you tap out your referral network pretty early by the seed stage.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You know, it's rare that people have that many cracked engineers in their network.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So that's the second problem, but the third is completely relates to Inbound.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Have you done any sort of Inbound hiring recently?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: No, no, we haven't done any hiring yet.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: We're super early.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: It's just a co founder, Ryan.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I just so Inbound is a fucking mess.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's an absolute mess.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And it's a mess because ATS systems are no longer able to differentiate between candidates.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: When the marginal cost of bullshit is 0 and an AI can perfectly fabricate a resume, it means resumes are completely pointless.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: A signal that combined with Auto Apply is, you know, effectively we're seeing folks get buried in applications, but founders are spending more time reading resumes and doing screenings than ever.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And that's the main sort of thing that, that we address or we, we sort of try to help folks with.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Do you have any sense of how much you're going to rely on inbound or are you going more with a referral based strategy?

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, referral.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Or even hiring a recruiter in the worst case.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, fair enough, Fair enough.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I mean, I think, I think those are all legitimate options if you're interested in a recruiter.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We have a bunch of recruiting is.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I don't know if.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Oh, yeah, we haven't announced this officially.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I don't think it's come out in the network newsletter.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But my company, Careerspan, we signed a deal with Marvin to be an official talent partner for them.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And so as part of that, all community members get, you know, first hundred applicants on the house.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: As far as going through Career Span, getting processed by Careerspan doesn't seem like you're too enthusiastic about Inbound.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And I will say there's a reason for that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Like, it's sucks.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But the thing that we do that's very different is the way in which applicants engage with Career Span is a lot more substantive and lends itself to better signal.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So amongst all of these different strategies that you're using, good hiring is like a diet.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And you want to make Inbound a meaningful part of the diet.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We help you do that without having to then also spend a ton of your time and Energy reviewing 99 resumes for one winner, which is really the problem with Inbound.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Cool.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Well, do you want to tell me a little bit more about how you guys work and.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So where Careerspan been?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Been at it for about two years.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Spent most of our existence as a career coaching product.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The pain we identified was that people don't need resume generators, they don't need interview help.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Really what they need is something to help them understand their value proposition.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: For most folks, the reason they suck at job hunting is their own inability to be good advocates for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The resume is a byproduct of your.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Is a, is a byproduct of your ability to self advocate.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So our tool uses used conversational coaching to help people develop that ability.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: In the process, what we learned is that that modality is actually really powerful at getting to know someone.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And the interview process is essentially a bad compromise between getting to know someone and evaluating them.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So by using Career Span, you get to get people to engage in this pretty cognitively intense process that gives you better insight into who they are.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But then also that all happens at scale with an AI.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So you only see the actionable insights off of all of that effort they're putting in.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Say that last two sentence again.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So you basically get the actionable insights, that is, why should I talk to this person?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Why shouldn't I talk to this person?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You get those insights, you know, by virtue of them having engaged in this more extended interaction with Career Span, which is something that you have to figure out when you read a resume.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: When you read a resume, you are problem solving and trying to figure out whether this person's worth talking to.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Our product gives you that actionable information with the reasoning traces so that if you wanted to justify that, you could look back and see why the AI is, is saying what it's saying.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Okay, got it.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: So if I understand correctly, basically for job hunters, you're offering a still to like help them develop their value proposition.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: And in the process of engaging with your platform to develop that value proposition, you basically create a sort of profile of this person, which then is used by hirers to assess or like to figure out who would be a good match.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Precisely.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: In fact, I can show you visually as well what that ends up looking like is actually this.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So in this account, I mean, very similar to an ATS ostensibly.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But what you get with Careerspan you don't get anywhere else is obviously summary of the candidate, but you get to see how many stories they've told.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Each story is about 15 minutes worth of effort, generally answering about 7 to 10 pretty detailed, thought provoking questions about your experience.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So if you can only imagine how much data that collects for you.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And then to my point about actionability, we're planning on renaming these, but this is essentially why should you say yes to Donna?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Why should you say no To Donna.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You can make a decision much faster off of this.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Oh, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I think someone wants to join the call.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Oh, thanks.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: That's my note taker.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah, of course.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So I assumed.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So you, you essentially get in these very actionable terms and I can actually spin up this account for you.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So we can make dummy accounts pretty easily.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I can spin up this account for you and get this out, but it, it sort of is able to give you this much higher grade of analysis.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The other thing you'll observe is these are the same person.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's just with this, it was only the resume and this is resume plus stories.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And so you can, you know, even if you just want to for now, take my word on the fact that 62 to versus 90 means something that shows you the delta in nuance data that we are able to bring to the table to help you refine your decision.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, interesting.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, very interesting.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The other reason why I wanted to talk to you earlier than even when you put out the job is, you know, let's face it, if you put this job out publicly, you give away your hand to your, your sort of competitors, you, you reveal your hand and the longer you have it out, the more resumes you have to go through.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Or you have to read.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's completely pointless.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: At the same time, wouldn't it be nice for you to have a pipeline, right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: At least a frame of reference to begin the search?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: With careerspan, you can have both.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You can put your job in as a stealth job onto our job board and just start aggregating users.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We're seeing generally about 100 hits a day, but we only launched the job board about two, three weeks ago.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So it's, you know, you're going to see that ramp up.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It'll be completely cloaked.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So you don't, you don't have to have any sort of additional work obligation arising and we will just collect up the candidates for you and just give them to you when you actually start looking.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So it's, it's a way to like trawl for candidates almost, you know, in the sea.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: And so you're that that phase is candidates applying to that stealth thing or is that you guys not revealing the job posting at all and you picking off people that you think are more most relevant.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The former, the former us putting up the job as a stealth job, making it clear that the hiring is going to begin, let's say in Feb.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That transparency is good, we recommend.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But the thing is, in any other dynamic, if it was just Submitting a resume, there's no real reason for the person to do that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: In the case of Career Span because we have a pretty highly engaged user base.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: On average, the activated CareerSpan user does two and a half stories.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That's almost.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah, that's almost 40, 50 minutes worth of self reflection that they do that drives the sort of matching experience.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And so what we've observed is because people already have those stories.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Anyone that's a good fit for your job.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: They can truly one click apply but you will still only see the personalized interpretation of why they aren't a fit.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Okay, very interesting.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Nice.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Are the folks using your platform primarily.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Geographically concentrated anywhere us Most of our candidates are unsurprisingly New York, Boston, sf and that's where most of our roles are as well.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We are seeing a lot of demand for remote roles.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So if you are posting a remote role, we generally see greater uptake for that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But we've got some awesome companies.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We've got.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Probably the coolest is like Zoe, they're a lightspeed backed startup, 8 million seed round.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: They just found their infrastructure engineer who is a. I love this story because this is just a guy in Bozeman, Montana, right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Just a guy in Bozeman, Montana applied through Career Span.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You know, no great shakes University would not have gotten a look in on any other platform.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: On Career Span, not only is he brought to the front of the table, it's good for Zo because they get an underpriced asset.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I'm sure we all like that when we're hiring, right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You know who, who doesn't want messy on a budget, right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That's, that's sort of what we're.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That's also part of the framing is like you get this money ballist aspect with your hiring.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You know the last thing I'll say.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And this is just good advice from the hiring perspective.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Studies show that 46% of hires fail by the 18 month mark.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: 90% of the reason they fail is a values mindset or soft skills mismatch.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And that probably relates to your own intuitions around hiring that it's the person that ultimately affects success.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's not the skills.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Usually we're pretty good at figuring out who can or can't do the job.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We're very bad at figuring out who can do the job for my team and my company.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And that's perhaps the most important thing that we can help you with.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: What percent of people fail by the 18 month mark?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: 89% according to the Leadership IQ Institute.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Wow.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Because if you think about it, our whole interviewing evaluation process, Leetcode whiteboard tests, we're very good at assessing skills, hard skills.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We're very bad at assessing vibes and fit and relatability.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The cool thing with us is that because we use a purely semantic evaluation process, you can say, hey, I want someone empathetic.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And our system will find someone whose stories exhibit that quality.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Quality, you know, that's.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: No one else can really offer that except us.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So, yeah, I think for.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: For those reasons we can, you know, put the job out there.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You get it for free anyways, until the first, like the 101st first candidate.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And we can just hoover up these folks for you.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And then, you know, the moment if you start seeing some folks you're interested in, great, you can talk to them.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But I wanted to come at you with something that was like, you know, next to zero effort and zero sort of additional obligation for you.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, very cool.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, that makes a ton of sense.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: I mean, I think.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Definitely curious to try it, right, and see.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Just see what's on the market and sort of stuff.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I'm going to send you something.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Look at it after your term sheet is signed.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Like, frankly, it isn't even worth thinking about until then.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I'll worry about the following up.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: If you're just okay with me prodding you in, you know, later in.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: In the year or start of next year.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But yeah, I would just love to be helpful and wishing you the very best of luck, man.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: If there's anything else I can clarify as well, let me know.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And if I can be helpful in any way, let me know.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, super.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: I mean, sounds super cool and really awesome.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Kind of different angle at hiring market that you.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: You guys are doing.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: So I love that.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: I think it's very smart.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, we'd love to, you know, as.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: As it becomes relevant for us, we'd love to do it.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Definitely start of next year is when that's going to be.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: So, yeah, please do follow up and yeah, would love to.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Whatever you share is always useful and then we'd love to try it early next year.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Sounds good.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Sounds good.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Well, you take care, Dan, and thank you so much for your time.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I'll catch you soon.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Yeah, likewise.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Well done.

utterances.speaker: Daniel Csonth
utterances.text: Ciao.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Take care.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Ciao.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Hi, Bridge.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: Hey, how's it going?

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Good, thanks.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: How are you doing? Well, Dan, give me one second. I am just getting back to my desk after making a coffee. How's your morning? Good.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: I'm actually in London, so it's already the afternoon here.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: Oh, nice. Do you live in London full time?

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Sort of. Bit unclear. I'm always on the go. My wife lives here, but.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: I'm in denial that I live here as someone that has lived the nomad life for at least a good part of, of, of his existence. Yeah, I feel that it's, it's home is where I am, is what I like to think, or where my loved ones are. So, you know, it's a flexible concept.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Yeah.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: Yeah. Well, hey, thanks so much for, for jumping on. It was, you know, really cool hearing about, hearing about your company during the McKinsey sort of founder time. And obviously you'd mentioned that you're growing really fast and so wanted to both be generally helpful and sort of understand, you know, what, what are the pain points maybe you're going through are the ways that I can help, either directly through my company or just indirectly, but then also, you know, it's a great learning exercise for me to speak to folks that are scaling up their teams.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Nice. Yeah, very cool. And by the way, I don't know if you have multiple cameras, but I see it from the side in case you have one.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: I do. Yeah. That is, Let me fix that. Hey, there we are. Are we there? There we are. Yeah. Yeah. These, these, these office setups, man, they're getting too, too elaborate these days.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Yeah, yeah, very cool. And look, I, I, I admit I didn't have time to, to look at your company because I'm actually in term sheet negotiations this week and it's been absolutely mental.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: Oh, man.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: So I'm like, just texting here with, with the lawyers in parallel.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: No, no. So understand that. How's that going, by the way? Are you feeling good about it?

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Yeah, we have a lead that we're pretty excited about, so.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: Amazing. Amazing. Oh, man, that's half the battle, right? Getting that first person to really stake out their, their support.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Exactly, exactly. So super exciting. And yeah, that means we're going to be hiring start of next year. Definitely. Interesting.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: What have you, what have you sort of planned out for that hiring so far? And how are you feeling about going into that hiring cycle?

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Yeah, we haven't, we haven't really planned out much other than like roughly the profiles that we need, which is like kind of an AI engineer front end Engineer and a full stack engineer.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: Sounds about right. Yep.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Pretty standard. Like first team.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: Yeah. Yep.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Set up. So that's, that's mostly what we need. Probably like some a part time UX designer or something like that. And then I'm going to handle most of the commercial stuff. Maybe like get a founder's associate, something like that as well to help me a bit. But yeah, that's, that's kind of it.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: That's the main ones. And are there any concerns, pain points I didn't note? If you're a second time founder, usually those folks are a lot more chilled out about hiring but wanted to get a sense of sort of where you're, what headspace you're in and sort of what if there are any sort of anxieties or concerns that are worth addressing?

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Good question. I mean, I think, you know, it's, it feels like it's a competitive market out there. It's hard to say, right. Like on the one hand it's super competitive. On the other hand a ton of people are being let go.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: So. And it feels paradoxical, right? It feels paradoxical that that would be, that would be the case. Do you have any, do you have a suspicion of why that might be?

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: Well, I think, yeah. I mean obviously like with AI, right, the engineers are becoming a lot more productive and actually the people that are getting let go of are probably the less good ones. Right. So it almost like makes it worse because the people that are going to be applying are going to be the one who, you know, it's not always true, right, that the people that let.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: Go are the ones.

chunks.speaker: Daniel Csonth
chunks.text: But like tendencies. There's probably something that the companies are doing where they're like assessing who to let go of and who to not.

chunks.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
chunks.text: So yeah, I would, I would largely agree with that. I would say that is, that is one of three things that I would point out to you. One is certainly that phenomenon, right, where obviously the less performant folks are the ones that are let go first second dimension is something everyone's familiar with. The best people aren't looking for jobs, right? They're passive and they getting through to them or getting eyeballs on them is naturally very hard. So that's the second dimension of a problem. The founders I speak to, it's like, hey, I know I have a cool company, I know I have a good job, I need to get better eyeballs on it. That's the sort of second dimension of the problem. Usually you tap out your referral network pretty early by the seed stage. You know, it's rare that people have that many cracked engineers in their network. Yeah. So that's the second problem, but the third is completely relates to Inbound. Have you done any sort of Inbound hiring recently? No, no, we haven't done any hiring yet.
